caveats: a: OEWS is at four-digit NAICS 488200, includes employers of all sizes, and excludes self-employed workers.
a: Occupation shares are not task shares; wage weighting and within-occupation exposure are judgmental.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis, so its vintage and basis differ from the 2023 occupation evidence.
rho: Evidence of automation at Class I railroads may not transfer to LMM service firms with lower data maturity and capital budgets.
rho: The estimate excludes demand, price, and valuation effects and applies only to the exposed opportunity in a.
e: NAICS is assigned at the establishment level, while the primitive concerns transferable firms.
e: The product list also includes handling and rental-related products that can create mixed business models.
e: The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a transportation-sector EBITDA margin and is quality ESTIMATE.
s5: The Census owner-age data are reference year 2018 and not rail-industry-specific.
s5: Public transactions are selected and often larger than the lens; unsuccessful sale processes and closures are not observed.
s5: The cited G&W transaction involved a repair-shop asset within a broader rail transaction, not necessarily a standalone LMM-firm sale.
q: The cited agreement is a large-company case and may be more durable than typical LMM contracts.
q: Billing practices vary materially across fixed-price jobs, time-and-materials repair, cost-plus work, and dedicated switching agreements.
q: The estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit and does not include implementation difficulty or volume changes.
d5: All-mode freight growth is not rail-mode demand and the horizon conversion is approximate.
d5: BLS employment combines productivity and demand and covers railroad workers across industries, not the full service basket.
d5: Coal, chemicals, agriculture, intermodal, passenger, repair, and terminal-support subsegments can diverge sharply.
o: The operator requirement differs across repair, inspection, switching, and freight handling.
o: Current Class I practice may not predict five-year adoption among smaller outsourced providers.
o: This primitive is separate from implementation difficulty in rho and from demand volume in d5.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy physical, often safety-critical operating, inspection, repair, maintenance, and handling services through work orders, recurring service arrangements, and sometimes long-term contracts with committed minimum values or volumes.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive railroad or industrial units, pure equipment and right-of-way lessors, manufacturers and rebuilders selling products rather than recurring services, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring outsourced rail switching and terminal support, railcar and track inspection, repair and maintenance, and rail-freight handling services to external railroads, railcar owners, shippers, and industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures the employment and wage-bearing occupation mix for NAICS 488200, not task-level AI exposure and not exclusively LMM firms. I map high exposure to billing, bookkeeping, customer service, scheduling, data entry, and general office tasks; partial exposure to management, engineering, inspection analysis, and supervisory documentation; and little direct substitution to hands-on repair, rail operation, construction, and freight handling. The mapping may overstate exposure where administrative work is tightly coupled to field judgment, and understate it where machine vision and planning tools automate portions of technical roles.
caveats: OEWS is at four-digit NAICS 488200, includes employers of all sizes, and excludes self-employed workers.
Occupation shares are not task shares; wage weighting and within-occupation exposure are judgmental.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis, so its vintage and basis differ from the 2023 occupation evidence.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study across independent railcar repair shops, industrial switching operators, and track-maintenance contractors, coded by task and current software use, would materially move this estimate.
a.rationale: The 2023 occupation mix was dominated by transportation/material-moving occupations at 41.27% and installation/maintenance/repair at 24.01%, while office/administrative support was 8.38%, management 8.41%, and business/financial operations 2.32%. The physical majority limits direct substitution, but meaningful document, dispatch, billing, planning, exception-triage, and inspection-analysis tasks remain exposed.

d5.bridge: BLS projects railroad-worker employment, not constant-price demand for NAICS 488210 services, to rise 1% from 2024 to 2034; BTS projects all-mode US freight tonnage, not rail support, to grow 50% from 2020 to 2050. I use the slower labor outlook as a lower anchor after efficiency gains and the broad freight projection as an upper demand tailwind, then compress both to five years. This may understate maintenance and inspection demand if regulation intensifies, or overstate it where coal traffic declines or Class I railroads internalize work.
caveats: All-mode freight growth is not rail-mode demand and the horizon conversion is approximate.
BLS employment combines productivity and demand and covers railroad workers across industries, not the full service basket.
Coal, chemicals, agriculture, intermodal, passenger, repair, and terminal-support subsegments can diverge sharply.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain current FAF 5.7.1 rail-mode projections for 2026-2031 by commodity, FRA fleet and inspection forecasts, and backlog/revenue data from a panel of independent repair, switching, and track-service firms.
d5.rationale: Rail-support demand should remain broadly stable to modestly higher: freight volumes and intermodal activity provide a tailwind, while rail infrastructure and rolling stock require recurring physical inspection, repair, switching, and handling. Efficiency initiatives and shifts in commodity mix limit growth. Regulatory safety work adds resilience but does not immunize every subsegment from traffic cycles.

caveats: NAICS is assigned at the establishment level, while the primitive concerns transferable firms.
The product list also includes handling and rental-related products that can create mixed business models.
The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a transportation-sector EBITDA margin and is quality ESTIMATE.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 51 estimated LMM-band firms using ownership, customer mix, recurring-revenue share, standalone financials, and parent-company status would directly resolve eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS product definition is largely outsourced rail operations support, inspection, repair, maintenance, and freight handling, which fits the lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive units, establishments embedded in larger rail groups, firms whose economics are primarily equipment or property rental, project-heavy construction providers, and businesses without transferable customer relationships. The frozen n of 51 is an estimated LMM-band firm count and is not re-estimated here.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 488210 ($2,762,578,636) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 488210 ($8,548,608,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $189,784 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 488210); class <5: 220 firms, avg revenue/firm $921,382 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $90,572 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 109 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,372,679 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $134,934 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,839,690 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $377,442 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 91 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,582,780 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $745,387 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $27,151,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,669,009 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $103,553,214 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,179,281 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 51

o.bridge: Census defines the service basket as physical switching, repair, inspection, maintenance, and handling, while FRA documents automated inspection as an input to manual inspection and corrective-action planning. These sources describe activities and current automation roles, not the five-year retained-operator share. I infer that software can reduce labor and triage work but cannot replace most accountable field execution. The estimate could be high if autonomous inspection and remote switching diffuse faster than expected, or low if safety rules keep human accountability stricter.
caveats: The operator requirement differs across repair, inspection, switching, and freight handling.
Current Class I practice may not predict five-year adoption among smaller outsourced providers.
This primitive is separate from implementation difficulty in rho and from demand volume in d5.
o.change_evidence: FRA final rules and waiver decisions on automated inspection, customer procurement plans for autonomous yards, and operator-level data on remotely completed inspections and switching moves would materially move this share.
o.rationale: Most year-five demand still requires an accountable service operator because railcars, track, signals, loads, and terminal movements are physical and safety-sensitive. Machine vision, automated geometry measurement, remote diagnostics, and optimized dispatch can change how work is performed, but defects still need validation and repair, freight must still be moved or handled, and regulated inspections need accountable organizations.

caveats: The cited agreement is a large-company case and may be more durable than typical LMM contracts.
Billing practices vary materially across fixed-price jobs, time-and-materials repair, cost-plus work, and dedicated switching agreements.
The estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit and does not include implementation difficulty or volume changes.
q.change_evidence: Review 30 representative customer contracts and renewal histories across repair, switching, track maintenance, and handling, recording pricing basis, escalation, open-book provisions, change orders, term, and realized gross-margin retention after productivity projects.
q.rationale: Physical rail-support services are commonly purchased through work orders or service contracts, allowing an operator to retain part of a productivity gain before repricing. A disclosed switching and maintenance agreement had a 15-year initial term and five years of minimum annual dollar requirements, showing that durable contracted revenue exists. Retention is limited by competitive rebids, negotiated customer sharing, transparent labor hours and materials, cost-plus arrangements, and the bargaining power of large railroads, railcar owners, and industrial shippers.

caveats: Evidence of automation at Class I railroads may not transfer to LMM service firms with lower data maturity and capital budgets.
The estimate excludes demand, price, and valuation effects and applies only to the exposed opportunity in a.
rho.change_evidence: Structured interviews with operations leaders at 15-20 independent NAICS 488210 firms, plus implementation data from rail maintenance-management and machine-vision vendors showing deployed seat counts, cycle-time changes, overrides, and audit findings, would materially move this range.
rho.rationale: Within five years, independent operators can implement copilots for work-order intake, estimates, invoicing, compliance-document preparation, parts planning, scheduling, and inspection-data triage. FRA reports automated inspection already used by all Class I railroads, but the technology informs manual inspection and corrective-action planning rather than eliminating field accountability. Integration with legacy systems, small-firm data quality, safety validation, cybersecurity, labor acceptance, and customer approval constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: The Census owner-age statistic covers responding owners of US employer businesses, not rail-support LMM firms, while three cited 2024-2025 transactions demonstrate control-transfer liquidity but do not provide a denominator. I translate a 51% age-55-plus owner share, tempered because age does not imply a sale and because some eligible firms are sponsor- or corporate-owned, together with observed strategic and sponsor acquisitions, into a five-year control-transfer probability. This can overstate transfers if aging owners close or pass firms internally, and understate them if consolidation accelerates.
caveats: The Census owner-age data are reference year 2018 and not rail-industry-specific.
Public transactions are selected and often larger than the lens; unsuccessful sale processes and closures are not observed.
The cited G&W transaction involved a repair-shop asset within a broader rail transaction, not necessarily a standalone LMM-firm sale.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from rail-sector M&A advisers and databases, matched to a verified list of eligible $1-10M EBITDA firms and supplemented by owner-age and succession interviews, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A broad US owner-age proxy indicates substantial succession pressure, and recent acquisitions span a founder-built independent repair network, a standalone repair company, and a railcar repair shop. The evidence supports real buyer interest but not an industry-specific event rate, so the range remains wide.

principal_driver: Recurring, regulated physical maintenance and operating work creates durable demand while leaving a focused layer of administrative and inspection-analysis labor open to AI augmentation.
principal_weakness: Most payroll is hands-on and safety-critical, and powerful customers may capture productivity gains through transparent billing and contract renewal.

sources.supports: Industry occupation and wage mix: 36,590 jobs; 41.27% transportation/material moving; 24.01% installation, maintenance and repair; 8.38% office/administrative support; 8.41% management; and detailed occupations including repairers, rail workers, clerks, inspectors, and handlers.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 488200

sources.supports: Defines rail-support products including switching, consolidation, interconnection, track repair and maintenance, rolling-stock repair, cleaning, regulatory inspection, and freight handling; also identifies rental-related products.
sources.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 4882: Support Activities for Rail Transportation

sources.supports: FRA says automated inspection is used by all Class I railroads and supports asset inventory, analyses, mapping, condition assessment, and risk analysis while informing manual inspection and corrective maintenance planning.
sources.title: Automated Track Inspection Program

sources.supports: FRA proposed requiring qualifying automated track geometry systems at specified frequencies on covered mainline and controlled-siding track, showing both technology adoption and continuing safety-governance constraints.
sources.title: Track Geometry Measurement System Inspections

sources.supports: 2019 ABS infographic for data year 2018 reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, 43% were 35-54, and 6% were 34 or younger.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Documents Sojitz's August 2024 acquisition of Washamerica/BW Services, a tank-car repair and qualification company established in 1967 and serving major US oil companies.
sources.title: Sojitz Acquires Full Ownership of U.S. Railcar Repair Company

sources.supports: Documents G&W's September 2024 acquisition of ownership of Central Gulf Railcar Services as part of a broader transaction and describes repair-shop capacity and services.
sources.title: Genesee & Wyoming Adds Mobile, Alabama, Railcar Repair Shop to Its Footprint

sources.supports: Documents a 2025 sponsor acquisition of a founder-built independent railcar repair and maintenance network with 13 US facilities and about 1,500 employees; describes regulatory compliance and requalification services.
sources.title: EQT to Acquire Eagle Railcar Services

sources.supports: Discloses a 15-year railway services agreement for haulage, switching, railcar and locomotive repair, inspection, maintenance-of-way, car management, and material handling, with minimum annual dollar requirements for five years.
sources.title: FTAI Infrastructure Inc. 2024 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Projects railroad-worker employment to grow 1% from 2024 to 2034, cites 6,600 average annual openings, and notes that intermodal activity supports demand while automated systems limit employment growth.
sources.title: Railroad Workers: Occupational Outlook Handbook

sources.supports: BTS/FHWA projected all-mode US freight tonnage to grow 50% from 2020 to 2050 to 28.7 billion tons under FAF5.
sources.title: Freight Activity in the U.S. Expected to Grow Fifty Percent by 2050

sources.supports: FRA describes an almost 140,000-route-mile network, significant annual maintenance investment, rail's 28% share of freight ton-miles, a 52% bulk and 48% intermodal carload mix, and expectations for continued freight and capacity needs.
sources.title: Freight Rail Overview

title: Support Activities for Rail Transportation
